 +// prepends to user messages (e.g. plan mode). The real user content follows
 +// the prefix, so we strip rather than skip the message entirely.
 +func cleanSyntheticPrefixes(content string) string {
 +	prefixes := []string{
 +		"Implement the following plan:",
 +	}

 +			return strings.TrimSpace(trimmed[len(prefix):])

+ // extractContentText extracts plain text from a message content field.
+ // The content field may be either a plain string or a JSON array of typed content
+ // blocks (e.g. [{type:"text",text:"..."},{type:"tool_use",...}]).
+ // When it's an array, all "text"-type blocks are joined with newlines.
+ // This handles the Claude Code JSONL format where IDE context tags (e.g.
+ // <ide_opened_file>) are injected as separate text blocks before the real question.
+ func extractContentText(content interface{}) string {
+ 	switch v := content.(type) {
+ 	case string:
+ 		return v
+ 	case []interface{}:
+ 		var parts []string

+ 			if m, ok := block.(map[string]interface{}); ok {
+ 				if t, _ := m["type"].(string); t == "text" {

 +// stripMarkdownHeading removes leading markdown heading markers (e.g. "# Title" → "Title").
 +// These are structural formatting, not meaningful slug content.
 +func stripMarkdownHeading(message string) string {
 +	trimmed := strings.TrimSpace(message)
 +	if len(trimmed) > 0 && trimmed[0] == '#' {
 +		trimmed = strings.TrimLeft(trimmed, "#")
 +		trimmed = strings.TrimSpace(trimmed)
 +	}
 +	return trimmed
++=======
+ // xmlTagsRe matches XML-style tag blocks including their content, such as
+ // <ide_opened_file>...</ide_opened_file> or self-closing <tag />.
+ // These are injected by IDE tools/system prompts and should be stripped before
+ // processing user message text for filename or display purposes.
+ //
+ // The regex has two alternations:
+ //   - <tag ...>content</tag>  — matched with (?s) so . spans newlines; uses .*? (lazy)
+ //     to consume the shortest content between matching open/close tags.
+ //   - <tag ... />             — self-closing tags.
+ //
+ // Known limitation: nested tags with the same name (e.g. <t><t>x</t></t>) will
+ // only match up to the first closing tag, leaving a dangling </t>. This is
+ // acceptable because IDE-injected tags are not nested this way.
+ var xmlTagsRe = regexp.MustCompile(`(?s)<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?>.*?</[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*>|<[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_-]*(?:\s[^>]*)?/>`)
+ 
+ // whitespaceRe collapses any run of Unicode whitespace into a single space.
+ var whitespaceRe = regexp.MustCompile(`\s+`)
+ 
+ // stripXMLTags removes XML-style tag blocks (and their content) from s and
+ // returns the trimmed result with normalized whitespace. This cleans up injected
+ // IDE/system context that precedes the real user message text while preserving
+ // word boundaries where tags appeared between tokens.
+ func stripXMLTags(s string) string {
+ 	cleaned := xmlTagsRe.ReplaceAllString(s, " ")
+ 	cleaned = strings.TrimSpace(cleaned)
+ 	cleaned = whitespaceRe.ReplaceAllString(cleaned, " ")
+ 	return cleaned

```text
   544→// findFirstUserMessage finds the first user message in a session for slug generation
```

```text
    59→
    60→// extractWordsFromMessage extracts up to maxWords from the message, handling various edge cases
    61→func extractWordsFromMessage(message string, maxWords int) []string {
    62→	if message == "" {
    63→		return []string{}
    64→	}
    65→
    66→	// Strip leading markdown heading markers before they get converted to "hash"
    67→	message = stripMarkdownHeading(message)
    68→
    69→	// Normalize unicode and remove accents
    70→	t := transform.Chain(norm.NFD, runes.Remove(runes.In(unicode.Mn)), norm.NFC)
    71→	normalized, _, _ := transform.String(t, message)
    72→
    73→	// Convert to lowercase
    74→	normalized = strings.ToLower(normalized)
    75→
    76→	// Replace special characters with their word equivalents
    77→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "@", " at ")
    78→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "&", " and ")
    79→	normalized = strings.ReplaceAll(normalized, "#", " hash ")
    80→
    81→	// Remove all punctuation and special characters, replace with spaces
    82→	// This regex keeps letters, numbers, and spaces
    83→	re := regexp.MustCompile(`[^a-z0-9\s]+`)
    84→	normalized = re.ReplaceAllString(normalized, " ")
    85→
    86→	// Split into words and filter out empty strings
    87→	words := strings.Fields(normalized)
    88→
    89→	// Take up to maxWords
    90→	if len(words) > maxWords {
    91→		words = words[:maxWords]
    92→	}
    93→
    94→	return words
    95→}
    96→
    97→// generateFilenameFromWords creates a filename from words, handling edge cases.
    98→// Enforces lowercase to guarantee filesystem-safe, consistent filenames regardless
    99→// of how the words were prepared by the caller.
   100→func generateFilenameFromWords(words []string) string {
   101→	if len(words) == 0 {
   102→		return ""
   103→	}
   104→
   105→	// Join words with hyphens and enforce lowercase as a safety guarantee
   106→	filename := strings.ToLower(strings.Join(words, "-"))
   107→
   108→	// Ensure no double hyphens
   109→	for strings.Contains(filename, "--") {
   110→		filename = strings.ReplaceAll(filename, "--", "-")
   111→	}
   112→
   113→	// Remove leading/trailing hyphens
   114→	filename = strings.Trim(filename, "-")
   115→
   116→	return filename
   117→}
   118→
   119→// GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage generates a filename based on the first user message content
   120→func GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(message string) string {
   121→	words := extractWordsFromMessage(stripXMLTags(message), 4)
   122→	return generateFilenameFromWords(words)
   123→}
   124→
   125→// GenerateReadableName creates a human-readable session name from a user message
   126→// The name is truncated to 100 characters at a word boundary and cleaned of excess whitespace
   127→func GenerateReadableName(message string) string {
   128→	if message == "" {
   129→		return ""
   130→	}
   131→
   132→	message = stripXMLTags(message)
   133→	if message == "" {
   134→		return ""
   135→	}
   136→
   137→	// Normalize whitespace: replace newlines and multiple spaces with single space
   138→	name := strings.Join(strings.Fields(message), " ")
   139→
   140→	// Truncate to reasonable length (100 chars) at word boundary
   141→	maxLength := 100
   142→	if len(name) <= maxLength {
   143→		return name
   144→	}
   145→
   146→	// Find last space before maxLength to avoid breaking words
   147→	truncated := name[:maxLength]
   148→	lastSpace := strings.LastIndex(truncated, " ")
   149→	if lastSpace > 0 {
   150→		truncated = truncated[:lastSpace]
   151→	}
   152→
   153→	// Add ellipsis to indicate truncation
   154→	return truncated + "..."
   155→}
   156→
   157→// appendRemainingParts appends the remaining path components from parts[startIdx:] to the
   158→// result path, skipping any empty components. This is used when we can't canonicalize the
   159→// rest of the path (e.g., directory doesn't exist or can't be read).
   160→func appendRemainingParts(result string, parts []string, startIdx int) string {
   161→	for j := startIdx; j < len(parts); j++ {
   162→		if parts[j] != "" {
   163→			result = filepath.Join(result, parts[j])
   164→		}
   165→	}
   166→	return result
   167→}
   168→
   169→// GetCanonicalPath resolves a path to its canonical form by resolving symlinks
   170→// and correcting case. This is important on case-insensitive filesystems like macOS
   171→// where "/Users/Foo" and "/users/foo" refer to the same directory but would produce
   172→// different hash values.
   173→//
   174→// The algorithm works by walking the path component by component, starting from the root.
   175→// For each component, it opens the parent directory and reads its actual entries,
   176→// performing case-insensitive matching to find the correct casing on disk.
   177→// This ensures that the returned path matches the real filesystem casing, which is
   178→// important for consistent hashing and avoiding subtle bugs on case-insensitive filesystems.
   179→//
   180→// Note: This can be slow for long paths or directories with many entries, since it
   181→// requires reading each directory's contents.
   182→//
   183→// If a component doesn't exist on disk, the remaining path components are
   184→// appended as-is, making this function safe to use with paths that don't
   185→// fully exist yet.
   186→func GetCanonicalPath(p string) (string, error) {
   187→	// Ensure we have an absolute, clean path
   188→	p = filepath.Clean(p)
   189→	if !filepath.IsAbs(p) {
   190→		var err error
   191→		p, err = filepath.Abs(p)
   192→		if err != nil {
   193→			return "", err
   194→		}
   195→	}
   196→
   197→	// Resolve any symlinks in the path to get the real physical path
   198→	// This is critical for ensuring consistent path hashing across different
   199→	// ways of accessing the same directory (e.g., through a symlink vs directly)
   200→	realPath, err := filepath.EvalSymlinks(p)
   201→	if err == nil && realPath != p {
   202→		// Successfully resolved symlinks - update p to the resolved path for case normalization
   203→		p = realPath
   204→	} else if err != nil {
   205→		// Symlink resolution failed (e.g., path doesn't exist yet),
   206→		// continue with the original path - we'll still normalize the case below
   207→		slog.Warn("GetCanonicalPath: symlink resolution failed, using original path",
   208→			"path", p, "error", err)
   209→	}
   210→
   211→	// Split into components
   212→	parts := strings.Split(p, string(os.PathSeparator))
   213→
   214→	// Start with root
   215→	result := string(os.PathSeparator)
   216→
   217→	// Skip empty first element (before leading /) that results from splitting "/foo/bar"
   218→	firstPartIndex := 1
   219→
   220→	// Build path component by component
   221→	for i := firstPartIndex; i < len(parts); i++ {
   222→		if parts[i] == "" {
   223→			continue
   224→		}
   225→
   226→		// Open current result directory to read its contents
   227→		f, err := os.Open(result)
   228→		if err != nil {
   229→			// Can't read directory - might not exist yet
   230→			// Append remaining path as-is
   231→			return appendRemainingParts(result, parts, i), nil
   232→		}
   233→
   234→		names, err := f.Readdirnames(-1)
   235→		_ = f.Close() // Best effort close, error doesn't affect result
   236→		if err != nil {
   237→			return "", err
   238→		}
   239→
   240→		// Find the correct case
   241→		found := false
   242→		for _, name := range names {
   243→			if strings.EqualFold(name, parts[i]) {
   244→				result = filepath.Join(result, name)
   245→				found = true
   246→				break
   247→			}
   248→		}
   249→
   250→		if !found {
   251→			// Component doesn't exist, append remaining as-is
   252→			result = appendRemainingParts(result, parts, i)
   253→			break
   254→		}
   255→	}
   256→
   257→	return result, nil
   258→}
   259→
   260→// debugBaseDirOverride allows overriding the base directory for debug output.
   261→// When set, debug files are written to this directory instead of the default
   262→// .specstory/debug/ location. This is set via the --debug-dir CLI flag or
   263→// the debug_dir config option.
   264→var debugBaseDirOverride string
   265→
   266→// SetDebugBaseDir sets the override base directory for debug output.
   267→// When set to a non-empty value, GetDebugDir will use this as the base
   268→// instead of the default .specstory/debug/ path.
   269→func SetDebugBaseDir(dir string) {
   270→	debugBaseDirOverride = dir
   271→}
   272→
   273→// GetDebugDir returns the debug directory path for a given session ID or UUID.
   274→//
   275→// The debug directory is used to store detailed JSON records and debugging
   276→// information for SpecStory CLI sessions. This is shared across all providers
   277→// (Claude Code, Codex CLI, Cursor CLI) to maintain a consistent debug structure.
   278→//
   279→// When debugBaseDirOverride is set (via SetDebugBaseDir), the directory structure
   280→// is: <override>/<sessionID>/
   281→// Otherwise: .specstory/debug/<sessionID>/
   282→//
   283→// Example:
   284→//
   285→//	debugDir := spi.GetDebugDir("abc123-def456")
   286→//	// Returns: ".specstory/debug/abc123-def456"
   287→func GetDebugDir(sessionID string) string {
   288→	if debugBaseDirOverride != "" {
   289→		return filepath.Join(debugBaseDirOverride, sessionID)
   290→	}
   291→	return filepath.Join(".specstory", "debug", sessionID)
   292→}
   293→
   294→// WriteDebugSessionData writes the SessionData as formatted JSON to the debug directory.
   295→// This provides a standardized, provider-agnostic debug output alongside provider-specific raw data.
   296→//
   297→// The file is written to: .specstory/debug/<sessionID>/session-data.json
   298→//
   299→// Returns an error if the debug directory cannot be created or the file cannot be written.
   300→func WriteDebugSessionData(sessionID string, sessionData *schema.SessionData) error {
   301→	if sessionData == nil {
   302→		return fmt.Errorf("sessionData is nil")
   303→	}
   304→
   305→	// Get debug directory
   306→	debugDir := GetDebugDir(sessionID)
   307→
   308→	// Create debug directory if it doesn't exist
   309→	if err := os.MkdirAll(debugDir, 0755); err != nil {
   310→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create debug directory: %w", err)
   311→	}
   312→
   313→	// Marshal SessionData to pretty-printed JSON
   314→	jsonData, err := json.MarshalIndent(sessionData, "", "  ")
   315→	if err != nil {
   316→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to marshal SessionData: %w", err)
   317→	}
   318→
   319→	// Write to session-data.json
   320→	filePath := filepath.Join(debugDir, "session-data.json")
   321→	if err := os.WriteFile(filePath, jsonData, 0644); err != nil {
   322→		return fmt.Errorf("failed to write session-data.json: %w", err)
   323→	}
   324→
   325→	// Log absolute path for debugging
   326→	absPath, _ := filepath.Abs(filePath)
   327→	slog.Debug("Wrote debug session data", "sessionId", sessionID, "path", absPath)
   328→	return nil
   329→}
   330→
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
   142→func TestGenerateFilenameFromWords(t *testing.T) {
```

```text
   573→// FileSlugFromRootRecord generates the slug part of the filename from the session
   574→// Returns the human-readable slug derived from the first user message, or empty string if none
   575→func FileSlugFromRootRecord(session Session) string {
   576→	// Find the first user message and generate slug from it
   577→	firstUserMessage := findFirstUserMessage(session)
   578→	slog.Debug("FileSlugFromRootRecord: First user message",
   579→		"sessionId", session.SessionUuid,
   580→		"message", firstUserMessage)
   581→
   582→	slug := spi.GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage(firstUserMessage)
   583→	slog.Debug("FileSlugFromRootRecord: Generated slug",
   584→		"sessionId", session.SessionUuid,
   585→		"slug", slug)
   586→
   587→	return slug
   588→}

   590→// ListAgentChatSessions retrieves lightweight session metadata without full parsing
   591→// This is much faster than GetAgentChatSessions as it only reads minimal data from each session
   592→func (p *Provider) ListAgentChatSessions(projectPath string) ([]spi.SessionMetadata, error) {
   593→	// Get the Claude Code project directory
   594→	claudeProjectDir, err := GetClaudeCodeProjectDir(projectPath)
   595→	if err != nil {
   596→		return nil, err
   597→	}
   598→
   599→	// Check if project directory exists
   600→	if _, err := os.Stat(claudeProjectDir); os.IsNotExist(err) {
   601→		return []spi.SessionMetadata{}, nil // No sessions if no project
   602→	}
   603→
   604→	// Collect all JSONL files in the project directory
   605→	var sessionFiles []string
   606→	err = filepath.Walk(claudeProjectDir, func(path string, info os.FileInfo, err error) error {
   607→		if err != nil {
   608→			return err
   609→		}
   610→		if info.IsDir() || !strings.HasSuffix(path, ".jsonl") {
   611→			return nil
   612→		}
   613→		sessionFiles = append(sessionFiles, path)
   614→		return nil
   615→	})
   616→	if err != nil {
   617→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to scan project directory: %w", err)
   618→	}
   619→
   620→	// Extract metadata from each session file
   621→	sessions := make([]spi.SessionMetadata, 0, len(sessionFiles))
   622→	for _, filePath := range sessionFiles {
   623→		metadata, err := extractSessionMetadata(filePath)
   624→		if err != nil {
   625→			slog.Warn("Failed to extract session metadata",
   626→				"file", filePath,
   627→				"error", err)
   628→			continue
   629→		}
   630→

   632→		if metadata == nil {
   633→			slog.Debug("Skipping warmup-only session", "file", filePath)
   634→			continue
```

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-03-18 10:14:46Z)**_

I now have a complete picture. Here is my analysis of all three conflicts:

---

## Conflict Analysis

### Conflict 1 — `pkg/providers/claudecode/provider.go` (lines 496–542)

These two branches added **different new functions** to the same insertion point just after `isSyntheticMessage`.

**HEAD (cursor-ide)** adds `cleanSyntheticPrefixes` — a function that strips boilerplate prefixes Claude Code prepends to user messages in Plan Mode (e.g. `"Implement the following plan:"`). Rather than skipping those messages entirely, it strips the prefix and returns the real user content.

**dev** adds `extractContentText` — a function that handles the Claude Code JSONL format where the `content` field can be either a plain `string` *or* a JSON array of typed blocks (`[{type:"text",...}]`). Cursor IDE injects context via extra text blocks, so this extracts and concatenates only the `"text"` type blocks.

**Key finding:** Both functions are *already used* in the code **outside the conflict region**:
- `extractContentText(...)` is called at lines 560 and 709
- `cleanSyntheticPrefixes(...)` is called at lines 566 and 713

**Recommendation:** Keep both functions. This is a purely additive conflict — there is no logical contradiction between them.

---

### Conflict 2 — `pkg/spi/path_utils.go` (lines 19–58)

Again, two different additions at the same insertion point.

**HEAD (cursor-ide)** adds `stripMarkdownHeading` — a small function that strips leading `#` characters from messages (e.g. `"# Plan: Replace Gemini Watcher"` → `"Plan: Replace Gemini Watcher"`). Without this, the `#` becomes the word `"hash"` in the generated slug.

**dev** adds `xmlTagsRe`, `whitespaceRe`, and `stripXMLTags` — regex-based XML tag stripping to remove IDE-injected context blocks like `<ide_opened_file>...</ide_opened_file>` from message text before it's used for filenames.

**Key finding:** Both are also *already used* downstream in the same file outside the conflict region:
- `stripMarkdownHeading(message)` is called at line 67 inside `extractWordsFromMessage`
- `stripXMLTags(message)` is called at lines 121 and 132 inside `GenerateFilenameFromUserMessage` and `GenerateReadableName`

**Recommendation:** Keep both. Again purely additive. The `regexp` import is already present.

---

### Conflict 3 — `pkg/spi/filename_test.go` (lines 252–281)

Two sets of new test cases for `TestGenerateFilenameFromUserMessage` added at the same position.

**HEAD (cursor-ide)** adds one case:
- `"Markdown heading produces clean slug"` — verifies `# Plan: Replace Gemini Watcher Polling` → `plan-replace-gemini-watcher`

**dev** adds five cases:
- `"Uppercase message produces lowercase filename"`
- `"All caps message produces lowercase filename"`
- `"IDE tag prefix before real message"` — tests that XML tags are stripped before generating the slug
- `"Only IDE tag with no real content"` — expects empty result
- `"Multiple IDE tags before real message"`

**Key finding:** All six test cases test distinct, non-overlapping behaviours. They are all compatible and valid given the combined resolution of conflicts 1 and 2.

**Recommendation:** Keep all six. The markdown heading test belongs first (matching HEAD), followed by the five dev cases — though the order is arbitrary.

---

## Summary

All three conflicts are **purely additive** — two parallel workstreams each added new functions and test cases at the same location. The correct resolution for all three is to include **both sides**. There is no code that needs to be discarded, and no logical contradiction to resolve.

Shall I proceed with applying all three resolutions?

---
